title: Shephelah

infobox.value: ([Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew_language "Biblical Hebrew language"): הַשְּפֵלָה, romanized: *hašŠəp̄ēlā*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'the Lowlands') or **Shfela** ([Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"): הַשְּׁפֵלָה, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *haŠfelá*), or the **Judaean Foothills**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (Modern Hebrew: שְׁפֵלַת יְהוּדָה, romanized: *Šfelát Yəhūdá*), is a transitional region of soft-sloping [rolling hills](/wiki/Rolling_hills_(geology) "Rolling hills (geology)") in south-central [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") stretching over 10–15 km (6.2–9.3 mi) between the [Judaean Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains") and the [Coastal Plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain").[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2)[[3]](#cite_note-KKLBrPark-3) The different use of the term "Judean Plain", as either defining just the Coastal Plain segment stretching along the Judaean Mountains, or also including, or only referring to, the Shfela, often creates grave confusion.

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [31°42′N 34°55′E﻿ / ﻿31.700°N 34.917°E﻿ / 31.700; 34.917](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Shephelah&params=31_42_N_34_55_E_type:region)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Lowland region in south-central Israel

| Shephela  שְׁפֵלַת יְהוּדָה ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"))  Shfela, Judean Foothills, Judean Lowlands | |
| --- | --- |
| [Region](/wiki/Region "Region") | |

| [Location of Shephela](/wiki/File:WV_The_Shfela_region_in_Israel.png "Location of Shephela") | |
| Coordinates: [31°42′N 34°55′E﻿ / ﻿31.700°N 34.917°E﻿ / 31.700; 34.917](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Shephelah&params=31_42_N_34_55_E_type:region) | |
| Geology | [Rolling hills](/wiki/Rolling_hills_(geology) "Rolling hills (geology)") |
| Dimensions | |
| • Length | 55 km (N-S) |

| Highest elevation | 460 m (1,510 ft) |

The **Shephelah** ([Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew_language "Biblical Hebrew language"): הַשְּפֵלָה, romanized: *hašŠəp̄ēlā*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'the Lowlands') or **Shfela** ([Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"): הַשְּׁפֵלָה, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *haŠfelá*), or the **Judaean Foothills**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (Modern Hebrew: שְׁפֵלַת יְהוּדָה, romanized: *Šfelát Yəhūdá*), is a transitional region of soft-sloping [rolling hills](/wiki/Rolling_hills_(geology) "Rolling hills (geology)") in south-central [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") stretching over 10–15 km (6.2–9.3 mi) between the [Judaean Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains") and the [Coastal Plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain").[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2)[[3]](#cite_note-KKLBrPark-3) The different use of the term "Judean Plain", as either defining just the Coastal Plain segment stretching along the Judaean Mountains, or also including, or only referring to, the Shfela, often creates grave confusion.

Today the Shfela is largely rural with many farms, but the cities of [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod"), [Ashkelon](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon"), [Rehovot](/wiki/Rehovot "Rehovot"), [Beit Shemesh](/wiki/Beit_Shemesh "Beit Shemesh"), and [Kiryat Gat](/wiki/Kiryat_Gat "Kiryat Gat") roughly surround it.

The Bible assigned land in the Shfela to the tribes of [Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah") and [Dan](/wiki/Tribe_of_Dan "Tribe of Dan").[[4]](#cite_note-Naʼaman2005-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Biblical references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shephelah&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biblical references")]

The Shfela is mentioned many times in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-je-7) In the [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version"), its name tends to be translated as 'vale' or 'valley'. The Shfela was the site of many biblical battles. During the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt"), hollowed out hills were connected to form elaborate bunker systems for the combat with the Romans.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shephelah&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/HaShfela.svg/330px-HaShfela.svg.png)](/wiki/File:HaShfela.svg)

The Shfela region within Israel. Right: cities surrounding it - Rehovot (north), Ashdod & Ashkelon (coast), Beit Shemesh & Kiryat Gat (east)

The Shfela is bordered to the northeast by the [Samarian Hills](/wiki/Samarian_Hills "Samarian Hills") and the north-south Afek Pass at [Rosh HaAyin](/wiki/Rosh_HaAyin "Rosh HaAyin")-[Antipatris](/wiki/Antipatris "Antipatris") (near the east-west [Ayalon](/wiki/Ayalon_Valley "Ayalon Valley") Valley) and [Rishon LeZion](/wiki/Rishon_LeZion "Rishon LeZion") in the northwest, and in the south by the northern [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") (the [Nahal Shikma](/w/index.php?title=Nahal_Shikma&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nahal Shikma (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%A0%D7%97%D7%9C_%D7%A9%D7%A7%D7%9E%D7%94 "he:נחל שקמה")] area).

The Shfela consists of fertile [rolling hills](/wiki/Rolling_hills_(geology) "Rolling hills (geology)").[[7]](#cite_note-je-7)[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) Topographically, it represents the transition from the higher and more rugged [Jerusalem and Hebron Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains"), whose foothills it forms, and the Coastal Plain.[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) About 60 km (35 miles) long in north–south direction and only 13 km (8 miles) wide, it is subdivided into two parts: the western "Low Shephelah", which starts at an altitude of ca. 150 metres above sea level and rises to no more than ca. 200 metres above the Coastal Plain, and the eastern "High Shephelah" rising to altitudes between 250 and 450 metres above sea level.[[3]](#cite_note-KKLBrPark-3)[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) In the upper part the valleys descending from the Judean Mountains are deeper, and they broaden once they reach the lower part where the riverbeds create larger spaces between the hills.[[3]](#cite_note-KKLBrPark-3) Where they reach the Shfela, the rivers can flow over substantial distances along the border between the mountains and the hills, forming longitudinal valleys.[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) Passage between the east–west and north–south valleys has dictated the communication routes throughout history.[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2)

In geological terms, the Shfela is a [syncline](/wiki/Syncline "Syncline"), i.e. it formed as a basin whose rock layers were folded downwards, but is part of the wider south Judean [anticlinorium](/wiki/Anticlinorium "Anticlinorium")-a regional formation characterised by upward folding.[[3]](#cite_note-KKLBrPark-3)[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) Typical to the Shfela are the [Senonian](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous")-[Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene") [chalky](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") formations.[[2]](#cite_note-EncJud-2) The soft Eocene chalk is known locally as *kirton*, which tends to build a harder upper [calcrete](/wiki/Calcrete "Calcrete") crust (*nari*), so that in the past people quarried the *kirton* while leaving the *nari* layer in place as a ceiling.[[8]](#cite_note-MurphyOConnor-8) Apart from using the extracted rock, they also utilised the generated underground hollows for different purposes (refuge, burial, storage etc.).[[8]](#cite_note-MurphyOConnor-8)

One of the major characteristics is hills formed of [marl](/wiki/Marl "Marl")-covered soft [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk"), as opposed to the [Judean Hills](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains") which are made of hard chalk and [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The valleys and lower areas contain soil with a high sand content, as well as large tracts of fertile areas.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Shephelah#Possible_confusion_with_the_Coastal_Plain._A_source_would_help. "Talk:Shephelah")*] Seasonal [swamps](/wiki/Swamp "Swamp") can develop during the [rainy season](/wiki/Rainy_season "Rainy season"). The southern part is made up of [loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess"), while north of [Ashkelon](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon") consists of [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Shephelah#Ashkelon_is_on_the_Coastal_Plain,_probable_confusion_between_Shfela_and_the_Coastal_Plain._A_source_is_vital. "Talk:Shephelah")*]

The Shfela has a temperate Mediterranean to [semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate").

A series of east–west valleys cuts the Shfelah into districts. From north to south, they are: the [Valley of Ayalon](/wiki/Ayalon_Valley "Ayalon Valley"), [Sorek Valley](/wiki/Nahal_Sorek "Nahal Sorek"), [Valley of Elah](/wiki/Valley_of_Elah "Valley of Elah"), Guvrin Valley, Valley of [Lachish](/wiki/Tel_Lachish "Tel Lachish"), and Valley of [Adorayim](/wiki/Dura,_Hebron "Dura, Hebron"). The biblical towns established there guarded settlements of the interior and took advantage of trade passing along this route. Ayalon was the primary access corridor to [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") along the ascent of [Horon](/wiki/Ancient_Canaanite_religion#Deities "Ancient Canaanite religion").

Caves are a major feature of the southern part of the Shfela, many of them bell-shaped such as those in [Beit Guvrin](/wiki/Maresha "Maresha").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History and archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shephelah&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History and archaeology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/PikiWiki_Israel_19061_Plants_of_Israel.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_19061_Plants_of_Israel.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_19061_Plants_of_Israel.jpg)

Shfela lowlands

Archaeological surveys in the Shephelah have found evidence of habitation during the Late Bronze period.[[10]](#cite_note-Grabbe2008-10) During the early Iron Age, the population of what has been widely believed to be a Canaanite enclave[[11]](#cite_note-11) between the rising centres of both coastal Philistia and the Israelite/Judahite highlands, went into decline, though a string of settlements survived on the eastern edge. In the Iron Age IIA–B, population growth resumed and by the 8th century BCE it was densely populated, not so much by natural growth but as a result of incoming settlers, beginning with the short-lived settlement at [Khirbet Qeiyafa](/wiki/Khirbet_Qeiyafa "Khirbet Qeiyafa"). The overall estimated numbers for inhabitants range from 50,000 to 100,000, over numerous sites such as [Tel Lachish](/wiki/Tel_Lachish "Tel Lachish"), [Azekah](/wiki/Azekah "Azekah"), [Tel Burna](/wiki/Tel_Burna "Tel Burna"), [Tel Zayit](/wiki/Tel_Zayit "Tel Zayit"), [Khirbet el-Qom](/wiki/Khirbet_el-Qom "Khirbet el-Qom"), [Tel Erani](/wiki/Tel_Erani "Tel Erani"), Tel Harasim and Tel Nagila. This colonization, together with the inhabitants of the Canaanite enclave, identified with the highland Israelite/Judahite culture, and its expansion coincides with the decline of Philistia.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the 8th century BCE, the Shephelah was the most densely populated region in the [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), but most excavated sites in the region were destroyed during the [Assyrian invasion of 701 BCE](/wiki/Sennacherib%27s_campaign_in_the_Levant "Sennacherib's campaign in the Levant"), leaving the area largely depopulated in the following century.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

During the decline and ultimate destruction of Judah by the [Neo-Babylonian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire "Neo-Babylonian Empire") in 587 BCE, the region was taken over gradually by the [Edomites](/wiki/Edom "Edom") and it became the core of what was known in Greek as Idumea. The Shephela flourished during the Hellenistic period, was strongly affected by the [First Jewish–Roman War](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War") (66–70)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and was largely depopulated of Jews as a result of the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") (132–136).[[14]](#cite_note-14) It flourished again in the [Byzantine period](/wiki/Byzantine_Palestine "Byzantine Palestine") and was the scene of one of the major battles during the [Muslim conquest of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") of the 7th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the late [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk") and early Ottoman periods, the Shephelah underwent a marked reorganization of settlement patterns, characterized by the contraction of permanent villages and the increased use of marginal landscapes for seasonal habitation, pastoralism, and limited agriculture. Archaeological evidence from sites such as [Horvat Midras](/wiki/Horvat_Midras "Horvat Midras") (Khirbet Drūsye) and [Khirbet Beit Lei](/wiki/Khirbet_Beit_Lei "Khirbet Beit Lei") indicates that abandoned ancient ruins were reused as temporary shelters and activity areas, reflecting adaptive strategies in a landscape shaped by political instability, changing administrative control, and security concerns.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Archaeological evidence from [Horvat Midras](/wiki/Horvat_Midras "Horvat Midras") includes a rare mold for casting lead musket balls, found concealed within a roughly built wall of a temporary structure dating to the late sixteenth or [seventeenth century](/wiki/17th_century "17th century"). The find indicates local production of ammunition and reflects the need for personal security in a rural landscape marked by instability and reduced administrative presence. Such evidence illustrates how communities in the Shephelah adapted to changing conditions through seasonal occupation and self-reliance during the Ottoman era.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shephelah&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [Claude R. Conder](/wiki/Claude_R._Conder "Claude R. Conder"), in *Tent Work in Palestine* (pub. Richard Bentley and Son: London 1878, p. [276](https://jupiter.ai/books/kwzJ/#page_276)), wrote: "The term *Shephelah* is used in the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud") to mean the low hills of soft limestone, which, as already explained, form a distinct district between the plain and the watershed mountains. The name *Sifla*, or *Shephelah*, still exists in four or five places within the region round Beit Jibrîn."
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-EncJud_2-7) ["Land of Israel: Geographical Survey. Shephelah"](http://www.encyclopedia.com/religion/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/land-israel-geographical-survey). *Encyclopaedia Judaica*. Thomson Gale. 2007. Retrieved 29 January 2017. "Topographically, the Shephelah represents a transition zone between the Coastal Plains and the Hebron and Jerusalem Mountains. It is relatively narrow – about 8 mi. (13 km.) – in proportion to its south-north extension – about 35 mi. (60 km.). Though they form the foothills of the Judean Mountains, the Shephelah hills differ from the former in almost all respects. Structurally, they constitute a major synclinal part of the south Judean anticlinorium, composed mainly of chalky formations of Senonian-Eocene origin. Hypsographically, the Shephelah consists of two parts: a western one (the "Low Shephelah"), rising to a height of about 600 ft. (200 m.) above the Coastal Plains, and an eastern one (the "High Shephelah") about 600 ft. (200 m.) higher than the former. On the north the Shephelah borders on the tectonically conditioned Aijalon Valley, one of the main natural approaches to the Judean Mountains. The Shephelah is a region of gently sloped hills separated by the confluents of the major rivers descending into the area from the Judean Mountains. At their entrance into the Shephelah, these rivers, and several of their tributaries, form relatively wide-floored valleys that run for a considerable stretch along the border between the hill and the mountain region. Passage between these longitudinal valleys is relatively convenient, and this natural communication channel has been very important throughout history."
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-KKLBrPark_3-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-KKLBrPark_3-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-KKLBrPark_3-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-KKLBrPark_3-3) [Jewish National Fund (KKL), *British Park – Scenic trails in Israel's Heartland*. Quote: "The Judean Plain is an intermediate region situated between the Coastal Plain to the west and the Judean Mountains to the east. The altitude of its hills varies from 150 to 450 meters above sea level, and geologically speaking, the plain is a syncline, i.e., a basin in which the layers of rock have folded downwards and sunk. [...] The plain consists of two distinct landscape units: the lower plain, to the west, where the hills are lower and separated by broad riverbeds; and the upper plain, where the valleys are deeper and the hills rise to a height of between 250 and 450 meters above sea level."](http://www.kkl-jnf.org/tourism-and-recreation/forests-and-parks/great-britain-park.aspx)
